retrieved_docs.title: Piaget -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The subsequent stage, the concrete operational stage, begins at 7 years of age and harkens the termination of the egocentric attitude displayed previously; this stage proceeds until about 11 years of age. In this period, a child learns to apply logical rules to concrete or tangible objects and mentally transform, modify or manipulate what he sees and hears. An essential skill developed in the concrete operations period is the child's acknowledgment of the concept of conservation, where values remain unchanged in the course of visual transformation. For example, the conservation of quantity is classically demonstrated when water from a short, stout glass is poured into a tall, slender glass. At this stage, the child unknowingly uses logic to deduce that the amount of liquid did not change, only the container that holds it. Over time, the child builds on the basic understanding of conservation to master concepts of greater complexity. Early in this stage, the child would understand that when play-doh is transformed from a circle to a square, the volume or amount of Play-Doh has not changed, only the shape. Around age 9, the child would understand that weight is also conserved even when the shape changes. Conservation is mastered when the child fully comprehends that the properties of an object do not change when the object undergoes a perceived transformation. Included in the basic understanding of conservation is the concept and application of cardinal numbers, or counting, to determine that an amount or quantity can be consistent even when spread over a larger area. A child developing this concept would compare two rows of five dots, one with the dots one centimeter apart and the other with the dots one inch apart, and understand that the amount or quantity of dots in each row is consistent, even when spread over a greater area. As the child’s understanding of conservation develops, he will also begin to reverse-engineer the logic rules. This “reversibility” falls into two categories: inversion and reciprocity. Refer back to the different-sized glasses with the same amount of water. When the child saw the water poured from the shorter glass to the taller glass and understood that the amount of water did not change, the child understood conservation. When the child is subsequently able to mentally invert the liquid from the taller glass to the shorter glass, or vice versa, and understand that the amount of water has not changed, he or she masters reversibility by inversion. A child masters reversibility by reciprocity when the child understands that the different glasses can hold the same amount of liquid because what one container loses in height, it makes up in width; and vice versa, what the slender container loses in width, it makes up in height. Concrete operations are not restricted to conservation and reversibility. Class inclusion, which is the child’s ability to categorize objects by shape or type, is a form of concrete operations, as are relations, which is the child’s ability to apply logical rules to a series of items with gradual changes, like categorizing objects from smallest to largest. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Piaget -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The sensorimotor period begins at birth and continues through the child’s first two years. During this stage, the child experiences the world via sensation (sensory) and movement (motor). The sensorimotor stage is further divided into 6 substages. Stage 1 includes inborn motor and sensory reflexes, like the sucking and palmar reflexes. Stage 2 observes the primary circular reaction in which an infant experiences an event and then attempts to repeat the action. Stage 3 is a secondary circular reaction when an infant repeats an action with a specific desired consequence or achieves an unrelated consequence. Stage 4 comprises the use of familiar means to obtain ends. It entails the deliberate planning of steps to meet a goal or objective. Stage 5 is a tertiary circular reaction in which infants experiment with their environment, using the properties of one object to manipulate another--e.g. using an experimental object, like a stick, to push a ball that then makes a noise. Insight characterizes stage 6, wherein the child observes other people manipulate the environment--i.e. alloplastic adaptation--to reach the desired goal; the child then imitates the action to enact his own desire on the environment. The culmination of stage 6 and the sensorimotor period is the child’s understanding of object permanence and the ability to acknowledge that objects have an existence independent of the child’s interaction with them. Classic examples of understanding object permanence include a child’s realization that when a parent leaves, the parent continues to exist, or the child’s attempt to recover a hidden toy, indicating the child’s understanding that the toy still exists outside of view. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Tendon Transfers -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure is typically performed in the operating room under general anesthesia. A tourniquet is placed high in the axilla, and the arm rests on a hand table. The operative equipment required includes a tendon passer and basic hand tray.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Two on the side of the stretcher, grasping the sheet placed over the slideboard One at the head of the bed, grasping the pillow and the sheet One at the far side of the patient, between the chest and the hips An additional one can be at the foot of the bed.

retrieved_docs.title: Grasp Reflex -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The grasp reflex, also known as the palmar reflex, is a primitive, prehensile, involuntary response to a mechanical stimulus present in a newborn. As the name implies, the action resembles a grasping motion of the hand. This reflex appears around 16 weeks of gestation and can be elicited in preterm infants as young as 25 weeks of postconceptional age (see Image. Palmar Reflex). [1] [2] [3] [4] To elicit the reflex, the infant is laid in a symmetrical supine and comfortable position while he or she is awake. [5] The examiner strokes the palm of the infant with his or her index finger. [4] [6] The response to this stimulus comprises 2 phases: finger closure and clinging. [4] The infant's fingers undergo flexion to enclose the examiner's finger (finger closure). The pressure applied to the palm produces traction on the fingers' tendons, leading to the clinging action. [7] [4] [7] The thumb is not affected by this reflex.

retrieved_docs.title: Principle of Tendon Transfers -- Indications -- Upper Extremity Tendon Transfers
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive research results have demonstrated the effectiveness of these techniques. [9] For example, results from a 25-year study involving 134 individuals with cerebral palsy undergoing 718 procedures showed overall improvement in upper extremity function, with the best outcomes observed in patients with fair to good voluntary control. Another study comparing surgical treatment, botulinum toxin injections, and ongoing therapy in 39 children provided results showing that the surgical group demonstrated more significant improvements in wrist strength and overall movement scores. Long-term studies' results have also shown excellent patient-reported outcomes, even when positional results were mixed. [10] For pronation deformities, isolated pronator teres rerouting has emerged as the most effective and straightforward method. However, care should be taken when performing transfers in older pediatric patients near growth acceleration to avoid secondary deformities. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hand Tendon Transfers -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In the setting a non-displaced distal radius fracture, integrity of the EPL can be tested by having the patient place their hand flat on a table, inability to lift their thumb off of the table is consistent with an EPL rupture.

retrieved_docs.title: Palmar Grasp Reflex(Archived) -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The palmar grasp reflex is a primitive, prehensile, involuntary response to a mechanical stimulus present in a newborn. This appears around 16 weeks of gestation and can be elicited in preterm infants as young as 25 weeks of postconceptional age (see Image. Palmar Grasp Reflex). [1] [2] [3] To elicit the reflex, the infant is laid in a symmetrical supine and comfortable position while he or she is awake. [4] The examiner strokes the palm of the infant with his or her index finger. The response to this stimulus comprises 2 phases: finger closure and clinging. The infant's fingers undergo flexion to enclose the examiner's finger (finger closure). The pressure applied to the palm produces traction on the fingers' tendons, leading to the clinging action. [5] The thumb is not affected by this reflex. The higher brain centers regulate the palmar grasp, though it is a spinal reflex. The afferent nerve fibers include the ulnar and median sensory nerves that supply the palmar surface. The spinal relay center is in the cervical spinal cord, and the efferent nerve fibers have the motor nerves that supply the hand's flexors and adductors. [6] [7] The premotor cortex, supplementary cortex, and cingulate motor cortex of the brain are implicated in controlling the grasp reflex through the spinal interneurons. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Pool Safety -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Parents who have a home pool should be counseled on fence safety. Fences are the most effective and proven way to prevent the drowning of young children. Fences should be at least 4 feet high. They should surround the pool, in a separate enclosure separating the pool from the house and the rest of the yard. The fences should not be chain-link as this is easy to climb. They should lack any footholds, handholds, or be near any lawn furniture that could be easily climbed. The fencing material should also be easy to see through. The vertical slats should have no more than 4 inches of space between them, and there should be no greater than 4 inches from the ground to the fence. The gate should be at least 54 inches off of the ground and out of child’s reach. The gate should be self-closing and self-latching and should open outward. When not in use, the pool fence gate should be locked. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigger Thumb -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Although rare, congenital trigger thumb can be easily overlooked as newborns and infants often naturally maintain a flexed finger and thumb position. Therefore, healthcare professionals in delivery rooms, newborn nurseries, and primary care settings should routinely examine the hands of newborns and infants and promptly communicate any concerning findings.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The provider on the far side of the bed pushes the patient. The 2 providers on the side of the stretcher shift their weight from front to back, bringing the patient with them by pulling the sheet. Meanwhile, the providers at the head and foot of the bed ensure that the patient is secured, lifting the head, shoulders, and feet, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Perform proper hand hygiene.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance -- Transfer from a Bed to a Wheelchair
retrieved_docs.content: When the patient transfers back to the bed from the wheelchair, the safest sequence of actions is positioning the chair at a 45-degree angle to the bed, locking the brakes, raising the footplates, and rotating the leg rests outward. Only after the correct sequence is performed can the patient scoot to the front of the wheelchair. When the patient scoots forward, the body is positioned over the feet. This allows the patient to stand more easily. Leaning forward or grasping the edge of the bed is likely to cause the wheelchair to tip forward. Assistance can be given to block the person's knees to provide additional support. [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance -- Log-rolling Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Always keep the patient's arms on the side of the body. Some recommend crossing the arms over the chest.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Explain what the patient can do to help the procedure (hands crossed over the chest, chin tucked, etc) and obtain necessary supplies.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Care Transfer Techniques -- Clinical Significance -- Slide Board Transfers
retrieved_docs.content: A transfer belt is placed around the patient’s hips/buttocks. The wheelchair is placed close to the bed, and brakes are applied. The armrest is removed, and the footrests must be swung away. The patient of the assistant places the sliding transfer board under the patient's buttock/leg. Placing the sliding transfer board under the patient's buttock/leg prevents the patient from falling off the board. Fingers should not be under the board to avoid pinching the fingers. The slide board should extend the distance between the bed and the wheelchair. The assistant places one knee between the patient’s knees and the other near the wheelchair's front, close to the other surface. The assistant holds the transfer belt and slowly slides the patient across the board. If a patient moves from a wheelchair into a car seat or a bed and the patient's clothing sticks to the surface fabric, place a plastic garbage bag over the surface to decrease friction when sliding. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative interventions that includes specific techniques such as Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position. Application of Therapeutic Agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or other therapeutic substances, while Manual Conversion of Position involves the physical repositioning of tissues, organs, or skeletal structures during a procedure. Both Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position are considered child concepts within Surgical Manipulation, illustrating the diverse range of actions that fall under this overarching surgical domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmechanical method of resuscitation encompasses manual techniques for reviving patients, and its specific child concept is إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), which represents the hands‑on approach used to restore breathing and circulation without reliance on mechanical devices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative techniques that encompasses several specific procedures. Among these, the Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or biologic substances to treat disease directly at the surgical site. The Delivery of vertex presentation describes an obstetric maneuver used during childbirth to assist the passage of a fetus presenting head‑first, often requiring careful manual handling to ensure safe delivery. Manual conversion of position denotes a technique in which a clinician manually repositions a patient’s body part or the entire patient to achieve optimal anatomical alignment for subsequent treatment or to correct malposition. Together, these three child procedures illustrate the diverse ways in which surgical manipulation can be employed to administer therapy, facilitate delivery, and adjust anatomical positioning, all of which are integral to effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent, Combined obstetrical version, and Manual conversion of position are all specific types of surgical manipulation. As child procedures of the broader category of surgical manipulation, each involves an operative technique aimed at altering a physiological condition during a medical intervention. Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication to achieve a therapeutic effect directly at the target site. Combined obstetrical version denotes a coordinated obstetric maneuver performed surgically to reposition a fetus to a more favorable presentation for delivery. Manual conversion of position describes a hands‑on technique used during surgery to adjust the patient’s or organ’s orientation to improve access or outcomes. Recognizing these procedures as subcategories of surgical manipulation helps clinicians select appropriate operative strategies, anticipate procedural requirements, and optimize patient care through targeted therapeutic and positional interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Internal obstetrical version and manual conversion of position are both specific procedures classified as surgical manipulation techniques. Each of these methods is considered a child procedure within the broader category of surgical manipulation, reflecting their role as specialized interventions used to alter fetal position during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent and delivery of vertex presentation are both classified as child procedures within the broader category of surgical manipulation, indicating that these specific interventions are considered subtypes of surgical manipulation in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2421
retrieved_docs.content: Antepartum Procedures is a medical category that includes, as its child, the subcategory Antepartum Procedures: Application, Insertion, Removal, indicating that the broader group of antepartum interventions is organized into specific actions of applying, inserting, and removing devices or treatments during the prenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: of the cardiovascular system, underscoring its role in blood‑pressure regulation. Coding of overexertion includes a child code for overexertion from prolonged static position; this condition is associated with body posture, which in turn is related to orthostatic hypotension. Intrathoracic procedures on the aortic valve or coronary arteries performed with cardiopulmonary bypass are mapped to extracorporeal circulation, linking heart‑valve and coronary‑vessel operations to the broader category of extracorporeal support technologies. Collectively, these diagnostic, therapeutic, monitoring, interventional, and rehabilitative interventions illustrate the extensive and integrated scope of cardiovascular‑system procedures in patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES serves as the overarching category that includes the domain of Surgical Manipulation. Within Surgical Manipulation, two distinct procedural actions are identified: Application of therapeutic agent and Manual conversion of position. These child concepts represent specific techniques employed during operative and anesthetic care, illustrating how broader surgical and anesthesia practices are organized into more precise manipulative actions. Recognizing this hierarchical structure aids clinicians in categorizing and planning operative procedures and anesthesia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “has child” is linked to withdrawal and rebound effects, which can manifest as rebound tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2828
retrieved_docs.content: Other physical therapy therapeutic procedures are classified as a child category within the broader group of Physical medicine procedure (procedure), indicating that they fall under the umbrella of physical medicine procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: reconstructions—are organized under “other procedures associated with delivery.” Overall, the framework interrelates antenatal/postnatal care, multilingual terminology, instrumental deliveries (forceps, vacuum, breech extraction), fetal‑head manipulations (manual rotation, conversion, versions), induction methods (AROM, membrane sweeping, therapeutic agents), and tissue‑repair procedures, providing a structured model for comprehensive maternal‑fetal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2907
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent and internal obstetrical version are both classified as child procedures of surgical manipulation, indicating that each represents a specific type of operative intervention. As child entities under the broader category of surgical manipulation, these procedures involve direct manipulation of tissues or organs during a surgical setting; the application of therapeutic agent entails delivering a medication or biologic directly to a target site, while internal obstetrical version involves manually repositioning the fetus within the uterus. Recognizing both as subtypes of surgical manipulation helps organize procedural taxonomy, supports accurate clinical documentation, and guides appropriate patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1917
retrieved_docs.content: المعالجة التنفسية is classified as a medical procedure on the respiratory tract, encompassing interventions that support or restore pulmonary function. Within this broader category, إيصال الأكسجين عبر غشاء خارج الجسم is identified as a child procedure, representing extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, a specialized technique that provides oxygenation and carbon‑dioxide removal outside the body. In clinical documentation, المعالجة التنفسية is also linked to the response category 关于预定行程的原因:印象:时间点:救护车运输:名义型, indicating that considerations of respiratory therapy may be recorded in the context of ambulance transport scheduling and related temporal impressions.

retrieved_docs.content: these interrelated entities illustrate a hierarchical, cross‑lingual organization of routine and assisted obstetric procedures, perineal‑trauma classifications, and maternal‑child care, providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for managing labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.
